text: The robotic surgical system is used to perform fenestration of a splenic cyst, with robotic arms manipulating instruments to access and open the cyst.
The patient is a fifty year old female with chronic left upper quadrant pain and postprandial fullness.
She had a history of asthma and morbid obesity.
A CT abdomen pelvis was obtained for her preoperative workup showing a thirteen centimeter splenic cyst with benign features.
This is a selection of the axial cuts from the CT scan showing an eleven by thirteen centimeter splenic cyst with benign features.
No other abnormalities of the abdomen or pelvis were identified.
The coronal CT scan images display a large splenic cyst, visibly displacing the surrounding normal splenic parenchyma.
Due to her persistent symptoms, robotic splenic cyst fenestration was recommended.
The video shows the placement of surgical ports on the patient's abdomen, which are used for robotic access to the splenic cyst.
Four 8mm robotic trocars are positioned across the upper abdomen, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon opens the splenic cyst using laparoscopic scissors, aspirating approximately 850 cc of brown fluid from the cyst cavity.
The robotic surgical system is docked to the patient, preparing for the robotic splenic cyst fenestration procedure.
The surgeon performs lysis of adhesions using robotic instruments, carefully separating the adhered tissues in the surgical field.
Dense adhesions are visible between the abdominal wall, liver, and omentum.
Endocyanine green dye is administered, highlighting the interface between the normal splenic parenchyma and the cyst capsule.
The surgeon marks the margin of the splenic cyst using a monopolar hook, preparing for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to follow the marked line, dividing the anterior cyst wall from the splenic parenchyma.
The surgeon encounters difficulty identifying the border between the capsule and the spleen at the superior medial aspect and applies indocyanine green (ICG) dye to visually delineate the margin.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide the final attachments between the cyst and surrounding structures, completing the separation process.
The surgeon irrigates the cyst cavity and evaluates it for hemostasis, ensuring there is no active bleeding within the cavity.
The surgeon identifies a bleeding vessel and treats it using a bipolar cautery device, effectively controlling the bleeding within the surgical field.
The surgeon irrigates the cavity once more to ensure hemostasis, visually inspecting the area for any signs of bleeding.
An omental patch is positioned to fill the cavity, and a 19 French Blake drain is placed within the surgical site.

The surgeon removes one of the eight millimeter trocars and enlarges the site to facilitate the placement of a laparoscopic retrieval bag, which is then used to extract the specimen from the surgical field.
Postoperatively, the patient was started on a regular diet.
The drain was removed on postoperative day three and the patient was discharged home.
Final pathology returned as dense fibrous tissue consistent with benign splenic pseudocyst.
At the time of follow-up, her left upper quadrant pain and postprandial fullness had significantly improved.
